Git Flow and commit conventions are usually a pain point for Git beginners — you rarely run into team collaboration early on. So this is a primer on what Git Flow is and why we bother with commit conventions.
Git Flow is a concept introduced in 2010. Heads up — it's a
The original article has this diagram:
I'll assume you already know what a Git Branch is, so each line in the diagram is a branch. The lines themselves aren't the point — the labels on them are:
GitHub renamed master to main starting October 2020. New repos default to main. See
These five branches are what Git Flow is about.
The main branch has two phases.
main is the "production" branch for everything. When you set up the project, you start from main. Once the initial setup is done, you move into the dev phase.
In dev phase, devs are forbidden from
committing or pushing directly to main
, because main is the production branch.
So once init is done, you cut a develop branch from main. From then on, develop only accepts merges from other branches and never receives direct commits. This matters.

develop is cut from main. It's the base branch for all development work. What does that mean? If you want to build a new feature — say, an "add article" feature — you cut a feature branch from develop (covered below).
main and develop usually live forever on the repo — they're called long-lived branches. Both should avoid direct commits. When a feature is done, you merge feature back into develop.
feature is cut from develop. One important thing to watch out for: don't just run
. If you push that to the remote, you might get pulled aside for a chat.
The reason: Git uses a tree-style structure for branches. Naming a branch literally
blocks others from creating any branch under that namespace. The right way is to use a slash and name the actual feature — for example, an upload feature would be
So in any real project, you'll see a pile of
Once a feature is done, it merges back into develop.

You might be wondering: do you delete feature branches afterward? Usually no — deleting kills the traceability of who built what. So feature branches usually stick around. (GitHub branches are free anyway.)
hotfix is a bit special. It's only used when a production bug hits the main branch. You cut it directly from main, and hotfix is the branch where you fix the production issue.
When the fix is done, you merge hotfix back into
. Why develop too? Because if you don't, when develop eventually merges back into main, the same bug comes back.
And typically you don't cut hotfix from develop — develop is the dev branch, and a lot of in-progress features that haven't been tested yet would get merged into main, causing worse bugs.

release is cut from develop. It's a pre-production testing branch — used when develop is about to merge back into main. develop cuts release, you test on release, and once tests pass you merge release into both main and develop (keeping them in sync). The release branch is also where you fix any last issues.
With that grounding, the diagram should make more sense now:

Honestly though, instead of more theory, I'd recommend contributing to an open-source project. Hands-on is much clearer. Git Flow is kind of abstract until you do it. Companies often have their own variations of Git Flow too, so what you actually use depends on the team's conventions.

Even with Git Flow keeping team collaboration sane (fewer conflicts, etc.), if you don't standardize commit messages, finding bugs becomes a pain. Code reviewers won't know what you did either — they'll have to open every file to figure it out. Without commit conventions you'll end up with a project that looks like this:
Even though I described what I did in each commit, in a team setting if everyone wrote commits like this, you'd have no idea whether "added dynamic route title" is a new feature or a bug fix.
So an AngularJS team published their commit conventions:
The AngularJS conventions define these types:
style - code style/formatting that doesn't affect logic (ESLint, missing semicolons...)
refactor - refactoring/optimization, neither a bug nor a new feature
With these prefixes, a reviewer can instantly see what kind of change a commit is. You can also approach each one differently (you don't review docs the same way as code, right?).
How do you actually use these conventions? Normally you'd run
— with conventions, you'd format it like
That's the lazy version. If you run
, you'll enter the commit editor (don't forget
Per the convention, you'd write something like:

# Test commit for this post
feat: add upload feature

express add to upload feature...

# Use this format to link a GitHub issue
issue #2
# Use this format to link a GitHub issue

Of course commit conventions vary by company.
Keeping all the commit types in your head is exhausting. I wrote a post on
, a tool that turns commit formatting into a guided prompt — so you can actually enforce conventions with less mental load.
